schools.summary: The setting full of 'heavenly sacraments': leaving Judaea for the Gentiles (Rabanus), the ship a type of the Church within which the word is kept while those 'without' cannot understand (Hilary), Jesus teaching from the waves what the people were unworthy to hear within (Jerome).
Henry: Christ unwearied, preaching both ends of the day (Eccl 11:6); in his humiliation he had no chapel of his own, his pulpit a ship — where Christ is, there is the church, though by the sea-side.
The 'Busy Day' (Robertson); he went out of Peter's house and sat by the sea of Galilee, the crowds along the shore; 'the same day' as the foregoing controversy.

themes: went out and sat by the sea

schools.summary: Multitudes gathered 'on divers accounts'; the Jewish custom that masters sat and scholars stood (Lightfoot); the boat seemingly kept for Christ's use (Lk 5:3); he sat in it because of the crush on the beach.

Calvin: the assembly eager to hear but not equally to receive; the parable shows the seed of doctrine is not everywhere productive, finding not always a fertile soil — so we need not wonder the Gospel yields no fruit in many.

themes: the whole multitude on the shore

schools.summary: A parable = a similitude from nature for spiritual truth, not necessarily a true narrative, to interest, arrest, rebuke (Nathan, 2 Sam 12:7), or conceal (Barnes); a form familiar among the Jews yet 'astonishingly improved' by Christ (Lightfoot, Clarke); the sower a figure from common life (Ellicott, Robertson).

Henry: Christ's parables borrowed from ordinary things, that spiritual things might slide into our understandings and common actions be spiritualized; Wesley: the sower 'exquisitely proper' as introduction, answering why the same seed has not the same effect.

outliers.note: The Catena material here (kingdom = grain of mustard seed, least of seeds grown to a tree) belongs to the mustard-seed parable (Mt 13:31-32), not the sower; so Jerome, Hilary, Gregory, Augustine here.

schools.summary: The wayside the hard, untouched path crossing the field, where the seed lay on the surface for the birds (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson); Lightfoot notes the paths through the sown fields (cf. Mt 12:1).

Henry: those who 'hear the word and understand it not,' an easy prey to Satan, 'the great thief of sermons,' who catches away the seed; Wesley: the order of hindrances — birds, scorching, thorns; if we break not the fallow ground and cover not the seed, we are the highway.

themes: some fell by the way side

schools.summary: A thin layer of soil over solid rock in the limestone country; the seed 'sprang up' sooner, not faster, for want of depth — the rapidity afterwards fatal (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson); Gill: hearts whose natural hardness continues.

Henry: hypocrites get the start of true Christians in profession, 'too hot to hold' — receiving with joy, without trying, swallowing without chewing; the heart may melt under the word yet not be melted by it; they endure 'but awhile,' not to the end (Mt 10:22).

themes: sprang up for want of depth

schools.summary: Scorched and withered for lack of root and moisture (Lk 8:6) — the over-rapid growth and underlying rock making the heat intense (Ellicott); the sun a figure of temptation or persecution (Gill), the word being first received with joy.

Henry: no root in themselves, no fixed principles — 'the green blade of a profession' without 'the root of grace'; when persecution arises 'he is offended' (Gal 5:11); the same sun that cherishes the rooted withers the rootless — 'lightly come, lightly go.'
The thorns' roots left below the surface, invisible at sowing, grew with the grain and slowly strangled it (Ellicott, Robertson); the field 'not cleared' of the briars within (Gill, Clarke).

Henry: the two thorns — the cares of this world ('a fruit of the curse... their end is to be burned,' Heb 6:8), which make men 'neglect the one thing needful'; and the deceitfulness of riches, when men rely on them — prosperity choking the word 'more dangerously, because more silently.'

Good, honest-hearted hearers made so by the Spirit (Gill); the hundredfold not an exaggeration but the mark of a prosperous harvest (Gen 26:12; Ellicott, Robertson); the differing returns imply differing degrees of fertility; Clarke marvels at grain's power to multiply.

Henry: fruitfulness distinguishes the good ground — intelligent hearers who understand their concern in the word and practise it; the ground has stones and thorns but none prevailing to hinder fruit; among the fruitful some exceed others, yet thirtyfold is graciously accepted.

A formula used for weighty, hard sayings, to quicken attention and stir a desire to understand (Gill); 'let every person who feels the necessity... pay attention' (Clarke); comforting that even Jesus had to exhort men to hear and understand (Robertson).
Calvin: a distinction among hearers — some have ears, others are deaf; and if any have ears, Scripture testifies it is the Lord who gives them (Ps 40:6), not man's own industry.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the baptismal anointing of the ears.

themes: who hath ears, let him hear

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the disciples asked privately (Mk 4:10) and for others' sake ('to them,' not 'to us'); 'to you it is given' implies no fate but that the unbelievers are the cause of their own misery; the blindness voluntary — 'seeing they see not' (Isa 6:9).

Augustine (at length): the Jews by prior sins deserved blinding, yet the parables' obscurity was in mercy, that humbled by the guilt of Christ's death they might turn with a healthier penitence (reconciling Jn 12:39).

Henry: the parable turns 'a dark side towards Egyptians... a light side towards Israelites' — 'a shell that keeps good fruit for the diligent, but keeps it from the slothful'; to the disciples it is given to know, the willingly ignorant losing the light.
The disciples, with Christ in the boat, asked in the pause after the parable why he taught thus, and why to plain peasants rather than the schooled (Ellicott, Robertson).

debates.question: Why did Christ teach in parables, and was the consequent blindness final or corrective?

sides.position: a voluntary blindness — the people the authors of their own loss
a judicial blinding for prior sins, yet in mercy unto a later penitence

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached ('more endurable,' belonging to ch. 11).

themes: to you it is given to know

schools.summary: 'Mystery' = a thing concealed but now revealed to the initiated, not something incomprehensible (Barnes, Ellicott; Rom 16:25); from mystēs, one initiated (Robertson) — the parables open to the disciples, shut to the hostile Pharisees.

Calvin: Christ spoke obscurely on purpose, that his word be a riddle to many; the word is bright in itself but choked by men's darkness; the comparison magnifies the grace given the apostles, which has no cause in themselves — God calls those he has freely elected.

A proverb of spiritual growth — he who improves grace has it increased, he who improves it not loses even what he has (Barnes); the same lesson as the Talents and Pounds (Ellicott; Mt 25:29); Clarke's Codrus who 'all that Nothing lost'; Wesley: the grand rule of God's dealing.

Calvin: God's gifts flow from his contemplation of his own liberality — those he begins to form he polishes to perfection (Ps 138:8); the reprobate proceed from bad to worse, their gifts shining only before men; aim at daily progress.

themes: whosoever hath, to him shall be given
from him that hath not it is taken

schools.summary: Matthew states the fact, Mark/Luke the intention ('that seeing they may not see'); no dishonesty — Christ stated his doctrine in the best way, the fault theirs (Barnes, Clarke); Ellicott: penal (answering 'taken away even that he hath'), yet with an element of mercy, for the parable could do no harm and might set men thinking.

Calvin: a veil spread over the blind, who are not partakers of the true light; he ascribes no blame here but commends the apostles' grace, assigning the cause only to the secret purpose of God; Wesley: more knowledge withheld because they use not what they have.

Isa 6:9-10, fulfilled in Isaiah's day and 'again fulfilled' in Christ's (Clarke); Matthew points out the fulfilment directly; the much-quoted text (Jn 12:40; Acts 28:26) dwelt on the minds of Christ and his disciples, preparing them for fruitless labour (Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin gathers the NT uses: Paul charges obstinate malice (Acts 28) and draws from the deeper source of election (Rom 11:7); the blindness voluntary, the blame wholly the people's, yet from God's hidden judgment; consolation for ministers whose labour fails.

'Waxed gross' = become fat, callous, even fatty degeneration of the heart (Barnes, Robertson); the eyes smeared/sealed, an oriental punishment (Isa 29:10); a wilful ignorance refusing to look lest conviction follow (Ellicott; Jn 3:19) — the fault wholly the people's (Clarke).

Calvin: 'healing' = deliverance from every evil, depending on the pardon of sins, since chastisement is mitigated when God is reconciled; yet repentance is not the cause of pardon but a mark of God's free favour — God forgives only those dissatisfied with themselves.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the freedom of the will, citing 'their eyes they have closed.'

schools.summary: A beatitude for the disciples against the blindness of the rest — happy to see truth others will not (Barnes); spoken to the small company in the boat, who had eyes that looked for light (Ellicott); Gill: blessed to see not only the flesh but Christ's transcendent glory as Son of God.

Calvin: their blessedness not the mere sight of the flesh but perceiving the glory of the only-begotten; no contradiction of 'blessed who have not seen' (Jn 20:29, rebuking Thomas), for believers in every age behold him in the Gospel face to face (2 Cor 3:18).

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on 'all things delivered to me' (Mt 11:27).

themes: the glory of the Son perceived
seeing by faith in the Gospel

schools.summary: The prophets 'men of desires,' each on a Pisgah height looking on a land not to be entered; 'have not seen them' only verbally at odds with 'Abraham rejoiced to see my day' (Jn 8:56) — the joy of the distant prospect implying desire (1 Pet 1:10; Heb 11:13).

Henry: a transcendent blessing desired but not granted to the saints of old, who had the types and shadows but longed for the Substance; their discoveries made 'according to the dispensation,' God's favours not anticipating his counsels — a spur to our thankfulness (2 Cor 3:7).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the many proofs of eternal life by faith and the gospel.

themes: types and shadows vs the Substance

schools.summary: Mark's gentle reproof, 'know ye not this parable?' (Mk 4:13), the simplest and the key to the rest; the emphatic 'ye' — the interpretation given to the disciples, withheld from others (Ellicott, Robertson).

Augustine, Jerome, Remigius dwell on the threefold fruit, dividing over its sense — virgins/widows/wedded (Jerome), the martyrs' degrees of contempt of death (Augustine), or faith/works/eternal life, or thoughts/speech/works (Remigius).
Calvin: four kinds of hearers, but Christ describes only those with 'some appearance of docility,' not open despisers — if the greater part of such perish, what of the rest of the world?

debates.question: What do the thirty-, sixty-, and hundredfold fruit denote?

sides.position: the wedded, widows, and virgins
degrees of contempt of death (martyrs at the hundredfold)
faith / good works / eternal life, or good thoughts / speech / works

themes: hear the parable of the sower

schools.summary: The seed the word of the kingdom (Mt 4:23); 'understandeth not' = regards not, lays not the heart to it (Clarke); the three Evangelists' three names for the enemy (wicked one, Satan, devil); the careless heart 'the devil's thoroughfare'; everything leading men to forget the truth does the enemy's work (Ellicott).

Calvin: the trodden soil receives no seed for want of preparation, hard as a pavement; yet men's depravity does not unmake the word — the Gospel is always seed 'as to its power, but not as to its produce'; the wicked one rushes to seize it before it springs up.

themes: heareth the word and understandeth not
the wicked one catcheth it away
seed in power though not in produce

schools.summary: The second hearer: rapid change, strong emotion, a quick show of conversion (Ellicott); caught with the gospel as new and pleasing, receiving it with joy and transport (Barnes, Wesley); the frequent fruit of systems that cause excitement.

Calvin: a 'temporary faith,' the seed vegetating and promising fruit, but the heart not subdued to the softness needed for nourishment — nothing done till faith acquires firmness, lest the alacrity vanish 'like a fire of tow.'

The 'root' is conviction striking deep into reason, conscience, and will (Ellicott); a mushroom growth quick to stumble (Robertson); not deeply convinced of guilt, he has no motive to counter the scandal of the cross (Clarke); tribulation and persecution distinguished as petty and organised.

Calvin: the cross lays open the vanity of the slightly-influenced; such imagine they have faith, rendering an honour that resembles it, yet are not regenerated by the incorruptible seed (1 Pet 1:23); Wesley: no change in the ground of the heart, finding pretences to leave the rugged way.

The double-minded hearer who would serve two masters — strength of purpose without unity of spirit (Ellicott); the blade and ear of partial reform but no full corn (Lk 8:14); 'lust for money and care' spoil many an earnest nature (Robertson); riches deceitful, promising peace they cannot give (Clarke).

Calvin: the sinful affections overcome faith before maturity, their influence unseen at first; each must tear the thorns from a heart filled with a thick forest of them, yet scarcely one in ten labours even to cut them down; riches called deceitful to make us guard against them (1 Tim 6:9).

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the cornfields and plucking (ch. 12).

schools.summary: Hears and understands — takes the word in with an honest and good heart (Lk), keeps it, and bears fruit; hearing, understanding, and fruit-bearing the three evidences of a genuine believer (Clarke); the soil may be improved — the preacher's work to prepare it (Ellicott; Jer 4:3); the fruit reveals the tree (Robertson).

Calvin: not the perfect, but those in whom the word overcomes every obstacle; some thorns always remain, yet whoever does not fall off is reckoned good soil; the degrees show believers are not equally fruitful, but the master owns even inferior soils — rejecting Jerome's virgins/widows/married as absurd.

The devil ever mixes error with truth, error coming after truth (false prophets after prophets, Antichrist after Christ; Chrysostom); the tares heretics, since the field is the world (Augustine); patience, lest a brother corrupt today grow wiser tomorrow (Jerome).

Calvin: the parable consoles the godly amid the confused mixture in the Church, arming them with patience; the toleration is of morals, not doctrine; against the Manichees, the wicked are not created by the devil but created by God and corrupted by him.

The field the world, the good seed the children of the kingdom (Henry, Barnes); the parable represents the present and future state of the gospel church — good and bad together now, separated hereafter.

debates.question: How are heretics and the wicked to be dealt with in the Church?

sides.position: restrain but do not put them to death — a never-ending war else
the Church may rightly constrain her lost sons (coercion defended)
tolerate immoral lives but never tolerate corrupt doctrine

themes: the kingdom likened to a man sowing

schools.summary: The 'tares' are darnel (zizania), a bastard/degenerate wheat indistinguishable from the true till the black grain shows (Robertson, Clarke, Lightfoot) — apt for hypocrites in the church, separable only by the Searcher of hearts; Satan sows false doctrine in darkness, often in a revival, then 'went his way,' knowing the soil (Barnes).
Gill and Clarke: the sleep of pastors and professors — lukewarmness and negligence — exposes the field; woe to indolent pastors who let the souls under them be corrupted.

The tares unnoticed till the wheat ripened — true piety and false hope known not by profession but by fruit (Barnes); 'Satan has a shoot of iniquity for every shoot of grace' (Clarke); at first all seems peace, love, and joy (Wesley).

Henry: secret wickedness long hid under a plausible profession breaks out at last; both wheat and tares lie under the clods, hard to distinguish, till a trying time, when fruit is to be brought forth, discerns the sincere from the hypocrite.

The servants the ministers of the Gospel, who, fearing for the wheat, spread the case before Christ in prayer (Gill); a faithful minister discovers the evil, laments it, and addresses God (Clarke); whatever is amiss in the church is not of Christ (Henry).

Henry: the rise of errors and scandals is matter of great grief to Christ's servants; sad to see such tares in the garden of the Lord; their first recourse is not to act but to enquire of the Master, coming 'with the heart and desire of the soul' (Remigius).

'An enemy hath done this' a plain answer to the origin of evil — God implanted no evil, the enemy did it with man's concurrence (Wesley); 'an enemy man' a Hebraism for a certain implacable foe (Gill); the Master does not blame the diligent servants (Henry).

Henry: the servants' wish to root up at once is over-hasty, inconsiderate zeal before consulting the Master ('wilt thou we call for fire from heaven?'); Clarke: rash zeal as much to be feared as the lack of discipline; Wesley distinguishes darnel (form without power) from open sinners (thistles to be rooted up).

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the sabbath and the temple (ch. 12).

themes: wilt thou we gather them up

schools.summary: The tares so resemble the wheat that gathering them would tread down and uproot the true (Barnes, Robertson); the householder a pattern of patient wisdom, choosing his time when harvest makes separation easy (Ellicott); no man can infallibly distinguish tares from wheat, so Christ would rather permit the tares than endanger the wheat (Henry).

Clarke: men often persecute a true Christian while meaning to prosecute an impious one; the zeal that persecutes for religious opinions is itself 'a seed of the devil'; the tares, continued under the means of grace, may become good corn — have patience with them.

themes: nay, lest ye root up the wheat

schools.summary: The harvest is the end of the world (Jewish state, the individual life, or the present order); hypocrites must be expected in the church, are the enemy's work, cannot be wholly removed, and over-zealous purging injures the wheat (Gill, Barnes, Robertson) — yet gross offenders may rightly be cut off (1 Cor 5:4-5).

Clarke presses the toleration to its edge against persecution: the minister may separate the incorrigible from fellowship but not punish in body or goods; the burning of heretics (Inquisition, Mary) is itself the diabolical seed, usurping God's seat of judgment.

debates.question: How far does 'let both grow together' restrain church discipline?

sides.position: discipline against gross, proven offenders is still a duty; toleration covers only unprovable hypocrisy
separate from fellowship but never persecute in body or goods; God alone judges false doctrine

themes: the harvest = end of the world

schools.summary: The kingdom's small beginning and vast growth; eastern mustard grows tree-like, the least seed apt for the church (smaller than any sect) and for feeble convert-faith (Barnes, Robertson); Ellicott takes the seed as the Christian society sheltering the nations (Ezek 31:3; Dan 4:10).

Calvin: Christ braces the disciples not to be offended at mean beginnings; the Lord opens his reign feebly so his power shines in unexpected progress — raise the mind to the God who creates from nothing (1 Cor 1:28).
Wesley: these fruitful-hearer parables; the kingdom is both the gospel dispensation and the inward kingdom (Lk 13:18).

outliers.note: Applies the mustard seed not to the church but to faith (Mt 17:20): small in size, fiery in operation, working the greatest effects in the soul.

themes: the church and the convert's faith

schools.summary: Largely philological: Lightfoot and Clarke document the proverbial smallness of mustard and the rabbinic reports of its great size (Sichin, Simeon ben Chalaphta); the description is popular, not exact — Robertson with Bruce calls it 'an excusable exaggeration in popular discourse,' Ellicott 'greater than the herbs' (Eze 17:23).

Allegorize nearly every term and openly differ: Augustine (leaven=love, woman=wisdom), Hilary (woman=synagogue hiding Christ by death; three measures=Law/Prophets/Gospels), Jerome (spirit/soul/body, or Father/Son/Spirit — while warning parables can't found dogma), Raban (love perfecting the soul).
Chrysostom: the leaven is the apostles, who change the whole world into their nature by being kneaded in, not destroyed — tribulation the very means of triumph; 'three measures' = great abundance.

Refuse the allegory: leaven, though usually a symbol of corruption (1 Cor 5:8; Mt 16:11), is here frankly a type of pervasive good (Ellicott, Robertson, who calls denial 'exegetical jugglery,' cf. 1 Pet 5:8; Rev 5:5); the working is inward/secret vs the mustard's outward growth; nothing is in the number 'three.'
Henry: leaven's certain, gradual, silent, unstoppable working is the manner of the gospel in world and heart (Mk 4:26-29; Ro 6:13).

debates.question: Is the leaven to be allegorized, and what are the 'three measures'?

sides.position: decode each term (leaven, woman, three measures) — though commentators disagree on the decoding
refuse allegory: leaven = pervasive influence for good; 'three' is merely the usual amount baked

schools.summary: He spoke in parables as they were able to hear (Mk 4:33), not to keep them ignorant but to provoke inquiry; yet none asked, so he departed (Chrysostom).
Jerome and Remigius preserve the textual controversy: the citation is from Asaph's psalm (Ps 78), not Isaiah; a copyist substituted the better-known name, which Porphyry exploited.

Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott: the quotation is Ps 78:2 (Asaph, a true prophet, 1 Chr 25:2); the 'Esaias' variant is a transcriber's error; from now parables dominate his public teaching while mysteries are reserved for the disciples.

Calvin: not that the psalm peculiarly predicts Christ, but the same majesty of the Spirit shone in his grave figurative speech; no conflict with Isaiah's prophecy, for in withholding light from the reprobate he yet accommodated their capacity to leave them inexcusable.

debates.question: Why does Matthew ascribe Psalm 78:2 to 'the prophet' (var. Isaiah)?

sides.position: original read 'Asaph the prophet'; a copyist replaced the unfamiliar name with Isaiah
the substance holds: Asaph ranks among the prophets and his words fit Christ's teaching

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the lost sheep and the sabbath (ch. 12).

themes: without a parable spake he not

schools.summary: The citation is Ps 78:2 (Asaph, 1 Chr 25:2), defended against the 'Esaias' variant; Ellicott shows Matthew's characteristic use of prophecy (cf. Mt 8:17; 12:17) — the psalm a historical survey, but the word 'parable' enough for an Evangelist who excluded chance; Robertson: 'I will utter' gushes like a river.

Calvin: not a peculiar prediction but the loftiness of figurative speech showing Christ handled hidden mysteries; he so regulated his style that even the reprobate, in their stupor, felt a divine power in it.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the eternal generation of the Son, citing 'Bethlehem' (Micah 5:2) and Abraham rejoicing to see his day.

themes: I will open my mouth in parables
things kept secret from the foundation
a divine power felt even by the reprobate

schools.summary: He spoke in parables to draw out questions; when none came he sent the crowd away and entered the house, where the disciples (not the scribes, who followed only to catch him) now ask freely, passing the plain parables for the harder tares (Chrysostom).

Origen: when with the multitudes Jesus is not in his house; it is his love of men to leave the house and go out to those who cannot come; the genuine hearer follows, inquires, and abides with him (Jn 1:35).

Clarke: those who attend only public preaching will not grasp the mysteries — one must come by secret prayer; Ellicott sets the scene in Peter's house; Robertson catches the aorist urgency 'explain right now' (cf. Mt 18:31); Barnes treats the whole interpretation, stressing the reality of hell against universalism.

themes: declare the parable of the tares

schools.summary: Origen extends the figure inward: the good seed are the children of the kingdom because whatever good is sown in the soul is sown by God the Word (Jn 1:1); while men fail to watch and pray (Mt 26:41) the devil sows evil opinions, and so the field is the whole world, not the church.

Calvin: the mingling of the wicked with believers is no accident but the devil's trick — charged on Satan, not God, who bears no blame for another's fault; and Christ says not the ministers but he alone sows, employing us as his hand while he alone is the Author of the work.

Ellicott: the parable explains the presence of evil in the Church, and only by analogy in the world; if the Son of Man is the Light of every man (Jn 1:9) he sowed good seed in the world from the beginning.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Christ going willingly to his passion and Judas's betrayal — belongs to another context.

themes: he that soweth is the Son of man
the devil's trick, not God's fault
Christ alone sows by his ministers
good and evil in the Church

schools.summary: Robertson stresses the Greek article on both nouns: the good seed AND the darnel are sown in the world, not the Kingdom or the church, separation coming only at the consummation (v.39); Gill widens the field from Judea to the whole earth (Ro 10:18; Col 1:6).

Ellicott corrects the temptation to equate the tares with heretics: heresy from self-will may be included, but the true definition is v.41 'all things that offend, and them which do iniquity' — a caution against persecuting heretics (Jn 8:44; 1 Jn 3:8).

themes: children of the kingdom / of the wicked one
not the church but the world

schools.summary: Ellicott: distinct recognition of a personal devil; the enemy sows 'while men slept' (security is danger); the 'servants' (pastors) differ from the 'reapers' (angels); the over-hasty zeal of Puritanism breeds worse evils, for tares may yet become wheat (Mt 25:31; Dan 7:9-10; 12:1-2).

Calvin: bear patiently the reprobate to the end, not to encourage pollution but to spare weak minds; the hasty rooters usurp the angel-reapers' office; against the Anabaptists, the same logic that allows excommunication allows the magistrate's sword (Mt 3:12; Jn 4:38; 4:35).

outliers.note: (implicit in source) note material on Tyre/Sidon mis-attached
Stray fragment on the signs of the end and the apostles' Olivet question.

schools.summary: Sparse: Gill simply rehearses the figure — the tares first gathered, bound, and burned in the fire prepared, so shall it be in the end of the world; the verse is the hinge turning the explanation toward judgment, reserving fuller treatment for vv.42-43.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the healed leper showing himself to the priest (Mt 8:4).

schools.summary: Robertson: 'out of his kingdom' = from the midst of the kingdom, since good and bad are mixed in every city (cf. v.49); the stumbling-blocks are not really members of the Kingdom but mixed in the field till the separation; Ellicott marvels the carpenter's son claims 'his' angels and kingdom, glossing 'all stumbling-blocks.'

Calvin: the offenders undermine many's faith, so be zealous not indolent against them, and not delicate (turning back at the smallest scandal); yet be confident, for the Son who bids us walk among scandals gives strength to overcome (Lk 13:27).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Christ's willing passion — misplaced.

themes: the Son of man shall send his angels
mixed in the world, not the Kingdom

schools.summary: The furnace is metaphorical, denoting the abiding wrath of God; alludes to Nebuchadnezzar's furnace or the burning of chaff, the Jews comparing hell to a furnace (Gill; Mt 3:12; Rev 19:20; 20:10); Ellicott: 'the' furnace of Gehenna with wailing and gnashing (Mt 8:12).

Calvin: as the glory laid up for the godly exceeds all sense, so the reprobate's punishment is incomprehensible and shadowed to our capacity; he philosophizes sparingly, content with the plain meaning, rebuking the Sophists' fruitless disputes and the curious who press every phrase.

The righteous shine forth (Dan 12:3); Robertson: 'shine out' as the sun from a cloud once the darkness is separated; Gill contrasts present reproach with future glory in Christ's imputed righteousness and the incorruptible body; Ellicott: the crowning blessing is the kingdom of 'their Father' (Prov 4:18).

Calvin: a remarkable consolation; the emphatic 'then' contrasts present obscurity with ultimate restoration; glory promised only to those already bearing God's image and advancing; the sun-comparison does not make all equal, for gifts variously given are variously crowned (Lk 17:21; 1 Cor 15:28).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the resurrection body, citing the same shining of the righteous — outside the parable's frame.

themes: the righteous shine as the sun

schools.summary: Allegorize the treasure variously: the gospel preaching hidden in the world, costly to possess (Chrysostom, Hilary); Christ the Word in the flesh or the Scriptures (Jerome; Col 2:3); the desire of heaven guarded from human praise (Gregory); the two Testaments (Augustine).

Henry: Christ himself the true Treasure (Col 1:19; Jn 1:16; Col 2:3), the gospel the open field where any may dig and search the Scriptures (Prov 2:4; Jn 5:39); those who discern its worth sell all to make it their own.

Barnes, Clarke, Robertson: a buried hoard or hidden mine; the finder's concealment does not square with strict integrity, but the parable (like the Unjust Steward) presses only his earnestness and total sacrifice (Lk 14:33; Php 3:8).
Calvin and Wesley read it inward: the kingdom within, hidden from the world's wise, found and hidden deep in the heart, all other happiness given up for it.

debates.question: What is the treasure, and the field?

sides.position: the gospel preaching / Scriptures / the two Testaments (allegorized)
Christ himself the Treasure, the gospel the field
a buried hoard or mine; the point is the earnest, total sacrifice, not the field's identity

themes: Christ / the gospel as the treasure

schools.summary: The one pearl variously: the truth, which is one (Chrysostom); the knowledge of the Saviour and his passion, beside which the Law and Prophets are dung (Jerome; Php 3:8); the sweetness of the kingdom (Gregory); Christ / love of neighbour / the Word (Augustine; Ro 13:9; Jn 1:1), the buying of which is the possession of ourselves.
Origen: the prophets are the mussels conceiving heavenly dew; the one costly pearl is the Christ, on whose finding all the rest are easily gathered.

Salvation worth all (Barnes, Clarke, who cites the Talmudic Joseph-and-the-pearl); Ellicott grounds the figure in Roman pearl-luxury (Mt 7:6; 1 Tim 2:9); the marked difference from the treasure is that the search is presupposed — the seeker of goodly pearls led to Christ (Php 3:8; Jn 7:17).

themes: Christ / the truth as the pearl

schools.summary: Brief: Robertson catches the vivid Greek tenses (dramatic perfect 'he has gone off and sold,' then bought it) and the word for a travelling merchant; the point is the totality and eagerness of the surrender for the kingdom's surpassing wealth.

Calvin guards against legalism: 'bought' does not mean men bring a price (Isa 55:1); the heavenly life is God's free gift, yet we 'buy' it by cheerfully relinquishing fleshly desires, as Paul counted all loss to gain Christ (Php 3:8).

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on Gentiles from Tyre/Sidon and kinsmen thinking him beside himself (ch. 12; 2 Cor 5:13).

themes: went and sold all and bought it

schools.summary: Chrysostom: unlike the tares (heresy) and the sower (inattention), here men are lost for evil life though drawn by the net (knowing God); the parable is added lest men trust in preaching only or think bare faith enough.

Jerome: the net is the gospel woven of the two Testaments, cast by the apostle-fishermen into the sea of the world (Jer 16:16); Gregory: the net is the Holy Church drawing every kind of man, full only when the human race is complete.

Robertson: the sagenè (drag-net, not the hand-net of Mt 4:18) sweeps in all, separation coming afterward; Barnes: Christ's own lips give the sharpest pictures of hell, against universalism (Mt 22:10); Clarke/Ellicott: the casting of the net may change the fish, so the 'bad' are so by their own will.

Calvin: partly the same design as the tares (remove the offense of a mixed church), partly distinct — to keep the disciples from resting in mere profession, since to be gathered into the fold avails nothing unless we are Christ's chosen sheep (2 Tim 2:19; 1 Cor 14:33).

themes: a net cast into the sea
mixture now, separation at the end

schools.summary: Brief: Gill reads the fulness ecclesiologically — the church-state full when all the called are gathered and the fulness of the Gentiles is in (Ro 11:25); Robertson notes 'vessels' (angè) occurs only here, distinct from the form in Mt 25:4.

themes: good into vessels, bad cast away

schools.summary: Gill rehearses the figure (good fish into vessels, dead cast away); Barnes stresses the severing is 'from among' the just (cf. v.41) and the angels' express ministry in it (Mt 25:32), as in the tares where Christ assigned the work to the angels.

Henry: the angels of heaven do what the angels of the churches (ministers) cannot — sever the wicked from the just; having commission and instruction from him who knows them that are his, there shall be no mistake either way.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the signs of the end and the apostles' question about Christ's coming — belongs to the Olivet discourse.

themes: angels sever the wicked from the just

schools.summary: Treated as a deliberate refrain: Gill and Barnes cross-reference v.42, Clarke his note on Mt 8:12; the dragnet ends where the tares ended, both parables closing on one note of solemn warning.

outliers.note: Stray fragment quoting the furnace-language of Mt 6:30 to refute Marcion — belongs to the catechetical lectures.

schools.summary: Jerome and Hilary: the disciples must not merely hear like the multitude but understand, as having to teach; the apostles are the Saviour's notaries, fittingly called scribes instructed (Song 7:13).
Origen: Christ, though knowing men's hearts (Jn 2:25), asks as a true man, using the human characteristic of inquiry, as God asked 'Adam, where art thou?' and 'Where is Abel?' (Gen 3:9; 4:9).

Barnes: the kind question — he was still willing to teach; the truth gained must not lie unemployed; Clarke: divine truths considered again and again till understood; Ellicott hears both grateful growth and child-like simplicity in 'Yea, Lord.'
Calvin: all the parables explained in private; Christ's object was not merely that they be informed but that they communicate to others what they received, whetting their desire for instruction.

themes: have ye understood all these things
Christ asks as a true man

schools.summary: Augustine: 'new and old' (not 'old and new') keeps the order of value, against the Manichaeans who keep only the new; Gregory rejects the two-Testaments reading (else Abraham/Moses were unlearned) for the kingdom's sweetness and punishment's terror, or both Testaments in word and life; Hilary: Law and Gospel from the Spirit's treasury.

Clarke: the scribe is the minister taught of God, whose treasury is Scripture, bound to distribute liberally and suit his discourse (the same sermon to all proves no scribe); Ellicott: one of the few sayings comparing Christ's and the apostles' work to the scribes', 'new and old' the secret of reverence and courage; Robertson contrasts the stale Rabbinical scribe with the fresh disciple of the kingdom.
Calvin and Wesley: the teacher lays up a store by long study to draw out, as from a granary, instruction fitted to every need (2 Tim 3:16-17).

debates.question: What do 'things new and old' denote?

sides.position: the two Testaments (Law and Gospel)
NOT the two Testaments but the kingdom's sweetness and punishment's terror, or both Testaments in word and life
a varied store of Scripture/doctrine fitted to the hearers (no fixed allegory)

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on a kingdom/house divided against itself (Mt 12:25).

schools.summary: Jerome marvels at the Nazarenes' folly — they wonder whence Wisdom has wisdom yet stumble at the carpenter's son, and so err over his 'brethren' (kinsmen, sons of the other Mary, guarding the perpetual virginity); Hilary and Pseudo-Augustine lift the carpenter to the Father framing all nature.

Chrysostom: 'his own country' is Nazareth, not Capernaum where the works were done (Mt 11:23); they ought to have honoured him the more that one of such parentage and no schooling spoke so wisely (as David from a husbandman, Amos a shepherd), proving his wisdom came of divine grace.

Henry: Christ came again to those who rejected him, not taking refusers at their first word; they upbraided his want of education and poor relations, turning against him what was really for him, for the unschooled who gave such proofs must be divinely commissioned.

Calvin and the harmonists: some interval elapsed (the notes of time not pressed; Lk 4:16; Mk 6:1); Nazareth's contempt persisted unreformed; familiarity bred contempt, and their unbelief, not any want of power, tied his hands (Lk 2:34; Isa 8:14).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Barnabas, Paul, and the disciples first called Christians at Antioch (Acts 11) — outside this passage.

themes: finished the parables, came to his country
the carpenter's son and his brethren

schools.summary: Origen: their wonder is unwitting testimony to a wisdom 'greater than Solomon' (Mt 12:42) and works greater than Elijah's, Elisha's, Moses', yet supposing him the carpenter's son they depreciated his kindred; notes the tradition (Gospel of Peter/Book of James) that the brethren were Joseph's by a former wife.

Barnes, Clarke: the surprise shows Jesus lived in obscurity till then; those who should know Christ best are often most ignorant of him; Wesley: many texts are missed for want of the proper emphasis — here 'whence hath HE' (Mk 6:1; Lk 4:16).

Calvin: willful blindness — they ought to have seen God's hand, but ingratitude dazzled them; compelled to admire yet despising, they reject a prophet God taught because men did not teach him, cutting their own throats by their very acknowledgment.

The trade taken plainly and turned to honour: Jesus worked as a carpenter till thirty (Barnes, against the infidel's sneer); the Jewish custom trained every child to a craft (Paul the tent-maker, Acts 18:3); Ellicott cites Justin on relic ploughs and yokes; Gill the Antioch schoolmaster's retort to Libanius.
Clarke: against Papist and Protestant prejudice alike, plain sense takes the named brethren and sisters as children of Joseph and Mary, Scripture being silent on the perpetual virginity (Mt 1:25).

sides.position: children of Joseph and Mary (younger half-siblings)
kinsmen / cousins, sons of the other Mary (Cleophas)
'brethren' is a Hebrew idiom for any relatives; Helvidius erred in inferring many sons

themes: is not this the carpenter's son

schools.summary: Light: Gill reports (from Epiphanius) the sisters named Mary and Salome and the two kinship views (Joseph's by a former wife, or daughters of Alphaeus/Cleophas) without adjudicating; Barnes refers back to v.55.
Henry's point (from the list): the sisters being 'all with us' and known to all was turned into contempt rather than respect — they should have honoured him the more as one of themselves.

themes: his sisters are all with us

schools.summary: The offense is pride and envy: rather than own a divine mission they suspect the devil, or stumble at his mean birth (Gill); men envy equals who claim uncommon wisdom (Barnes, with the Columbus parallel); it was unpardonable in Jesus not to be commonplace (Robertson; Mt 11:6; Jn 6:42; Isa 53:3).

Calvin, with Chrysostom: the proverb, though perhaps general, is applied to the Galileans, who should have been first to accept grace but drove him off; the more familiarly God shows himself, the more boldly men reject his Spirit (Jn 4:44; Lk 4:24).

debates.question: Is 'a prophet without honour in his own country' a universal proverb?

sides.position: not universally true (Elijah, Jeremiah were not dishonoured at home); true only figuratively, or of the Saviour singularly
a general proverb, here applied with peculiar reference to the Galileans
Christ may mean himself alone — less received among his own relatives

schools.summary: The power was not absolute but conditioned on faith, so will and power were alike thwarted (Barnes, Ellicott); yet he healed a few sick folk (Mark), leaving them inexcusable; Robertson: their disbelief blocked his will and power to cure; Gill: the few works became stumbling-blocks through their hardness.

Origen marks 'he could not,' not 'he would not': the working power receives co-operation from the faith of its object, hindered by unbelief (the disciples' little faith, Peter sinking, Mt 17:20; 14:31), while the woman with the issue, only reasoning a touch would heal, was healed at once (Mt 9:21).

Calvin: faith is the open mouth of the vessel, unbelief the stopper; yet 'not many' not 'none' — Christ's goodness strove with their malice and triumphed, opening a path where none existed; Henry: unbelief the great obstruction, the want being in us, not in Christ's power (Mk 9:23; Ro 1:16; Eph 2:8).

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the unpardonable sin and blasphemy against the Spirit (Mt 12:31).

themes: did not many mighty works there
faith the condition of the cure
